data.title: Vertex posts additional trial data for type 1 diabetes therapy
data.description:
Vertex Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:VRTX) has posted new data from a Phase 1/2/3 study for zimislecel (VX-880), its experimental therapy for type 1 diabetes, noting that the cell-based therapy achieved the main goal in trial subjects with at least one year of follow-up.

The Boston, Massachusetts-based biotech disclosed the results on Friday at the ongoing American Diabetes Association (ADA) annual conference in Chicago as part of an oral presentation.

The FORWARD-101 clinical trial was designed to evaluate the one-time infusion in T1D patients with impaired hypoglycemic awareness and severe hypoglycemic events.

Citing data from 12 patients who had at least one year of follow-up in Phase 1/2 portion of the trial, the company said that all of them achieved the primary endpoint related to the elimination of severe hypoglycemic events with at least a 7% reduction in the diabetes biomarker HbA1c.

While two patients died with no connection to the experimental therapy as previously reported, Vertex (NASDAQ:VRTX) indicated an encouraging safety profile for the drug, noting that most adverse events were mild or moderate and there were no serious adverse events.

“The magnitude, consistency, and durability of the results from all 12 patients with more than one year of follow-up reinforce the transformative potential of zimislecel for people living with T1D complicated by severe hypoglycemia,” said VRTX’s medical chief, Carmen Bozic.

The company said it has completed enrollments and dosing in the study while plans are underway to submit regulatory filings next year.

data.title: Novo updates Phase 1/2 trial data for novel obesity therapy, amycretin
data.description:
Novo Nordisk (NVO) has released full data from a Phase 1b/2a trial for its novel obesity drug, amycretin noting that the injectable, designed to target GLP-1 and amylin receptors, caused up to 24% of weight loss on average over 36 weeks with once-weekly dosing.

Early this year, the Danish drugmaker posted topline data from the study indicating that amycretin generated up to 22% weight loss over 36 weeks.

Both measures assumed that all patients adhered to the treatment regimen without any discontinuations, meaning the readout was based on a per-protocol analysis, which can lead to bias.

The trial was designed to evaluate once-weekly amycretin in 125 people with overweight or obesity over 36 weeks across three different maintenance doses.

However, noting that patients had not reached a plateau in weight reduction at the end of the trial duration, the company said amycretin can lead to additional weight loss if treatment duration is extended.

As for safety, Novo (OTCPK:NONOF) said the incidences of treatment-emergent adverse events increased with dosing, and amycretin indicated a side effect profile consistent with those found in early-stage trials for drugs targeting GLP-1 and amylin receptors.

The trial results were presented at the ongoing American Diabetes Association Scientific Sessions. Simultaneously, the medical journal The Lancet published data from a Phase 1 trial for amycretin in patients with overweight or obesity.

data.title: SA Asks: What lies ahead for the solar industry? (Update)
data.description:
(Adds comments from Seeking Alpha analyst Robert J. Lake.)

The solar industry has been facing significant headwinds from fluctuating trade policies and proposed changes for certain tax credits under the Trump-backed spending bill making its way through Congress.

We asked Seeking Alpha analysts Oliver Rodzianko, Victor Dergunov and Robert J. Lake for their takes on what lies ahead for this emerging sector. 

Oliver Rodzianko: Residential-focused solar stocks like Sunrun (NASDAQ:RUN) and Enphase (NASDAQ:ENPH) face the most near-term pain due to the abrupt elimination of residential investment tax credits, or ITCs, while utility-scale firms like First Solar (NASDAQ:FSLR) and Array Technologies (NASDAQ:ARRY) face more manageable risks due to the phasing out of ITCs. 

I'm a First Solar (NASDAQ:FSLR) shareholder, and I'm treating my investment as a short-term cyclical play. Early phasing out of utility-scale ITCs actually means there could be more near-term demand as developers rush to lock in favorable prices with producers. This is a time to readjust take-profit targets and to not forget the inherent cyclicality of solar power investment returns.

In the long run, of course, solar power will continue to dominate the energy landscape with strong and growing demand, but that trajectory will be volatile, aligning with shifting policy and the macroeconomic climate.

Victor Dergunov: The solar industry is in a lot of trouble here. Rates remain high with no easing in sight. High home improvement loan rates are keeping demand less than optimal. In addition, tariffs are impacting margins, and the U.S. government's phase out of solar tax credits will further impact demand moving forward. 

We need lower interest rates, but we are already seeing prominent bankruptcies in the solar segment and more will likely materialize. Many companies may not make it, but the ones that remain may grow to dominate the solar industry in future years and decades. Several companies I like in this space include First Solar (NASDAQ:FSLR), Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) and Enphase (NASDAQ:ENPH).

Robert J. Lake: Global energy demand is continuing to grow at a rapid pace, due in part to sustained population growth, the electrification of industry sectors, and the rapid expansion of AI and data centers. Global energy demands show no signs of slowing, and experts suggest a mix of different energy sources are necessary to meet demand.

Solar benefits from being both cheap and quick to deploy, with low maintenance costs, and experts suggest it will become the largest proportion of renewable energy, overtaking hydro, wind and nuclear, by 2028. Solar grew 29% globally in 2024 according to Ember.

China remains the undisputed leader in solar installations, breaching the 1000 GWh mark in early 2025, representing more than half the world’s solar capacity. Utility-scale solar is growing faster than rooftop solar, and technological improvements in energy storage are making intermittency less problematic.

In the U.S., the Senate panel’s proposed full phase-out of solar and wind tax credits by 2028 is by no means guaranteed to pass, and seems ill-advised, considering America’s energy demands cannot be met cheaply without the benefits of cost-effective and rapidly installed solar energy, and that most solar projects are in Republican states like Texas. The long-term prospects for solar are excellent, but there will remain short-term volatility due in part to cyclicality and shifting government policies.

I see various opportunities for contrarian investors in this space, especially in companies like First Solar (NASDAQ:FSLR) and Enphase (NASDAQ:ENPH) that focus on utility-scale installations and have significant business outside the U.S. On the contrary, I see SunRun (NASDAQ:RUN) as quite exposed for the opposite reasons: focused mainly on residential solar and the U.S. market. As picks and shovels plays in the solar industry, I maintain my bullish stance on NexTracker (NASDAQ:NXT) and Shoals Technologies (NASDAQ:SHLS).

data.title: Week of Israel-Iran fighting lifts oil as supply disruption fears push geopolitical risk premium
data.description:
The top crude oil benchmarks posted solid gains for the week, as escalating hostilities between Israel and Iran and concerns over potential supply disruptions in the Persian Gulf pushed prices higher.

Traders feared a worst-case scenario at the start of the week, in which Iran could shock the global economy by cutting off ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, keeping the risk premium at a high level even though such an event is considered unlikely, and in any case, the U.S. economy is much less dependent on Persian Gulf oil than in the 1970s.

Israeli strikes have disrupted Iran's domestic energy infrastructure in recent days, but exports from Iran have continued flowing to oil-hungry buyers in China and elsewhere, with Iranian shipments ferried abroad looking as much as 30%-40% higher than typical volumes at this time of year, according to Rystad Energy.

Oil futures fell on Friday after the White House said President Trump will decide in the next two weeks whether the U.S. will get involved in the Israel-Iran air war, and the U.S. Treasury Department issued new sanctions on Iran, which some analysts took as a signal the Trump administration may be trying to resolve the dispute in ways other than conflict.

"Although a major escalation is yet to occur, risks to supply from the region remain high, still hinging upon the potential for U.S. involvement," Tradu.com senior analyst Russell Shor said in a note. "The short-term bias is still tilted upward amid ongoing Middle East tensions, and the market will be watching crude intently until there are clear signs of de-escalation."

Brent crude fell sharply on Friday while WTI ended only slightly lower after the Juneteenth holiday in the U.S. caused a lag in updates, with Brent's sharp move in the previous session widening the discrepancy between the two benchmarks more than usual.

The Brent-WTI divergence also was influenced by the expiration of the July WTI contract on Friday, prompting traders to roll positions into the more actively traded August contract.

Both benchmarks rose for the third straight week, with front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for July delivery closing +2.7% to $74.93/bbl and front-month August Brent crude (CO1:COM) ending +3.7% to $77.01/bbl this week; on Friday, WTI and Brent settled lower by 0.3% and 2.3%, respectively.

Front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for July delivery finished +7.4% at $3.847/MMBtu this week, primarily due to expectations for hot weather forecast for much of the U.S. over the next two weeks, especially in the East Coast, where many record highs likely will be broken.

The Israel-Iran conflict has boosted global diesel prices, with gains outstripping the jump in crude prices, highlighting the vulnerability of diesel-heavy European consumers.

U.S. ultra-low sulfur diesel futures (HO1:COM) surged for the third straight week, with the front-month July Nymex contract +7.7% to $2.5418/gal this week, while front-month gasoline (XB1:COM) for July delivery +4.6% to $2.3295/gal for the week.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

Energy stocks, as represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA:XLE), rose 1% this week.

Top 20 gainers in energy and natural resources in the past 5 days: Houston American Energy (HUSA) +107.3%, Robin Energy (RBNE) +82.7%, Nine Energy Services (NINE) +74.5%, Geospace Technologies (GEOS) +73.3%, Battalion Oil (BATL) +69.9%, Critical Metals (CRML) +54.6%, Dawson Geophysical (DWSN) +50.3%, Indonesia Energy (INDO) +42.9%, Perma-Pipe International (PPIH) +42%, TMC the metals company (TMC) +36.7%, Mexco Energy (MXC) +34.4%, Marine Petroleum Trust (MARPS) +30.6%, MP Materials (MP) +29.2%, Green Plains (GPRE) +21.9%, Fluence Energy (FLNC) +20.2%, Centrus Energy (LEU) +19.9%, Comstock Resources (CRK) +19%, Mesa Royalty Trust (MTR) +17.4%, Darling Ingredients (DAR) +17.1%, Par Pacific (PARR) +16.3%.

data.title: Apple has internally discussed acquiring Perplexity AI - report
data.description:
• Hours after Bloomberg reported that Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) held talks with Perplexity AI, the news service is now reporting that Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) also had internal discussions about acquiring the artificial intelligence search startup.
  • Apple's head of M&A Adrian Perica has discussed the idea with SVP, Services Eddy Cue, along with other AI officials at the company, people familiar with the matter told Bloomberg. 
  • The talks within Apple are still at an early stage and may not result in a formal bid.
  • If Apple does acquire Perplexity AI, it would help the former develop an AI-based search engine. Apple currently has a deal with Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) to have the latter as the default search engine in its products, though that partnership is at risk from antitrust authorities.

data.title: Teck Resources weighs expanded production of chipmaking-metal germanium
data.description:
Teck Resources (NYSE:TECK) is considering options to boost production of germanium, a strategic metal key to chipmaking, and is talking with governments including Canada and the U.S. on available funding, a company executive told Reuters on Friday.

"We are examining options and market support for increasing production capacity of germanium," VP of communications and government affairs Doug Brown said in an interview.

China, which supplies ~60% of the world's refined germanium, restricted exports of the metal as well as gallium and antimony to the U.S., further escalating trade tensions following the Trump administration's crackdown on China's chip sector.

Teck (NYSE:TECK), currently North America's biggest germanium producer and the fourth largest in the world, is exploring ways to add to the current processing line using existing technology as one of the options, Brown told Reuters.

Most of Teck's (TECK) germanium, a by-product of zinc ore concentrate at its Red Dog operations in Alaska, goes to the U.S. after smelting and refining in British Columbia; Canada's germanium exports to the U.S. are exempt from tariffs as they comply with the USMCA trade agreement.

data.title: Orion Properties receives unsolicited offer to go private
data.description:
• Orion Properties (NYSE:ONL) said it has received an unsolicited offer from Kawa Capital Management to acquire the outstanding shares of the company it doesn't already own for $2.50 per share.
  • Kawa currently owns ~9.7% of outstanding shares of common stock.
  • Orion, which operates as a REIT, said its board of directors will review the bid with its legal and financial advisors.
  • In after-hours trading, Orion is up ~6% to $2.14 per share.
